How to Brew Better Coffee at Home

Most people dramatically overestimate how much gear matters and underestimate how much technique matters. Before you spend hundreds of dollars on a new machine, spend a week paying attention to three things: how fresh your beans are, how consistent your grind is, and how hot your water is when it actually hits the grounds.

Freshness is the single biggest lever. Coffee is at its best between four days and three weeks after roasting, and almost nothing you buy from a supermarket shelf falls inside that window. Find a local roaster, buy whole beans in small quantities, and store them in an airtight container away from light. That one change will improve your cup more than any piece of equipment you can buy.

Grind consistency comes second. Blade grinders chop beans into a mix of dust and boulders, which means part of your brew is over-extracted and bitter while another part is under-extracted and sour, all in the same cup. Even an inexpensive burr grinder produces a far more uniform particle size, which is why it is the one purchase almost every barista recommends first.

Buy a burr grinder before you buy anything else. Everything downstream of the grind is easier to control.

Finally, water temperature. Off-boil water scorches lighter roasts, and water that has cooled too far under-extracts everything. Aim for the range between 90 and 96 degrees Celsius. If you do not own a thermometer, let boiling water rest for thirty seconds before you pour, and taste your way from there.

  • Buy freshly roasted whole beans in small batches.
  • Grind with a burr grinder just before brewing.
  • Brew with water between 90 and 96 degrees Celsius.

Editor's note: this guide focuses on technique that applies to every brew method, from French press to pourover to drip machines.

Comments

Great post! I bought a burr grinder last month and it changed everything.
